speaker: interviewer
text: October 17th, 1996, interview with Jacob Wiener, tape number three. You were talking about when you were released from Wurzburg prison. Before we get to that, do you know how many people were arrested when-- when you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyone who was over 14 or 16 years of age, but people up to 60. Over 60, they didn't get arrest that at time. They didn't send to prison at that time. I don't know how many there were. A lot of people, it was filled up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you were released?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I ran downstairs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans are very methodical. I had to sign out, and I had to sign that they treated me like a king. [LAUGHS] And [CLEARS THROAT] of course, that happened once before. I don't know if I should mention it that once before when I was in the Nazi school, we were shown a certain film. And before we were shown the film, anyone-- everyone-- every student had to sign that they will never talk about this film, to no one in the world that they saw this film. This was a film about insane asylums in Germany. You see it in the museum. You see what they did with these people. They destroyed them. And in this film they told us there are 300,000 people in Germany who can never be of any worth and value for us, because they are-- they are mental-- mental kind of illnesses. They cannot walk. They are disabled, and so we have to destroy them. And we want to destroy them. Such people cannot be a [NON-ENGLISH], a member of the German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here also we had to sign [COUGHS] that everything went OK. So they gave me back all these things which I had. And they said to me, within 24 hours, you have to go back to your home city, Bremen, and report to the Gestapo within 24 hours. It was 7 o'clock in the evening. [CLEARS THROAT] So [CLEARS THROAT] first, I tried to go to the school. I didn't know what had happened during-- during that time, because we had no newspaper, no radio, and of course, television didn't exist. [LAUGHS] [COUGHS] So I went there, and I saw it was completely dark. And there was no one there. So I went a little bit back, and I met-- and you always have to have some luck. I met former-- the secretary of there. I met the secretary of the school. And I said to her, I have to go back to Bremen, and I have no money. So she gave me 20 marks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was-- then I somehow got entrance into the museum. I think she opened up. And I met another fellow there, and we took our things out, what we had. I didn't have very much. I don't even know if I took anything. And I went to the [CLEARS THROAT] railway station in Wurzburg. And the railway station was filled with Nazis and with all kinds of people. In the middle of the night, 10, 11 o'clock, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I bought a ticket. And I went to Bremen. [CLEARS THROAT] It takes about five, six hours, I think, to get to Bremen. But I wasn't sleeping during that night. I was just walking around in the train, because I didn't want to make myself conspicuous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. And I arrived in Bremen about early in the morning, maybe just about day time. In November in Germany, it's already very dark, and much darker than here at this time. [CLEARS THROAT] And I went there. So I called up from the station my home. There was no answer. Of course, I didn't know what had happened. Then I took a streetcar and I went to close to my home. And I went to my home in Bremen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw the window was smashed, and it was barricaded with wood. And we had two entrances to our building. One was to the store, and one was to our private quarters. So I saw there was a piece of paper there. And it said, get the key from the police station. [COUGHS] So I went to the police station, which wasn't very far, and I got the key. And when I came back to open the key-- a neighbor of ours opposite from where we had our store, he had a furniture business, three flights. And he was a good friend of ours all the time. He even wrote to my father after the war. [CLEARS THROAT] And he was standing in the door and was just-- [SUMMONS WITH FINGER] showing me with his finger to come over. He was afraid to go out and talk to me. Just showed with the finger 'come over to talk to you.' So I went over to him. And he said to me, I want to tell you something what happened here during the last week. [CLEARS THROAT] But I don't want anyone to hear. So he took me to a very innermost room. And he didn't even want his wife to hear and his two daughters whom he had. And he told me. I tell you-- last week the Nazis came here, and they broke into your house. And they killed your mother. [CLEARS THROAT] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But he said, two women came and buried her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to my father? He said, your father fled. He's on his way to Sweden. What happened to my other brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other brother went to get a doctor, and I don't know what happened to him. My sister was in Hamburg at that time. And your other brother, they took to concentration camp. He didn't know any more. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to myself, is there anyone here who I can talk to? No. So I said, then I go to Hamburg, because my sister was in Hamburg. So I took a train later in the afternoon, and I came to Hamburg, which was about two or three hours by train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stopped at the-- they have different stations. They have different railway stations. I stopped at the railway station which was closest to where my sister was. I knew that she was in one of the homes. At those times, girls couldn't do very much. She could only be, at the most, working with the children or doing some cooking in Jewish homes, not in other homes. So that's what my sister did. She was only 14 years old at that time. [CLEARS THROAT] So [CLEARS THROAT] I went to these people. And I knocked at the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was already twilight in the evening. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And when-- when they opened the door, I saw they were all standing there. [CLEARS THROAT] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they were very surprised to see me, because they were just making [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what that is? [INAUDIBLE] So Friday evening had just came at this time. They were very [? fun. ?] And we had some contact with this family till recently. Recently, the mother died at 92 years of age. And [CLEARS THROAT] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they asked me what-- where is my father? He said, he came here somehow, and he's staying in one of my mother's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] relative's house. And they were sitting shiva. You know what shiva is? But explain it for the tape, because people watching it might not know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shiva means that after someone dies, you stay for seven days in the house and say certain prayers. Shiva, the word 'shiva' means 'seven.' [POINTS] [LAUGHS] And kiddush means they make sanctification over wine for the Shabbat day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over there, and I saw my father there. Because what the man had told me, our neighbor-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- that he had intended to get to a Swedish ship and to leave Germany. But somehow, he made it there. And my brother, Benno, the second one, was still in the concentration camp Sachsenhausen, which is near Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the younger one, [CLEARS THROAT] he was going to a doctor. And the Nazis had gone after him, and he was afraid. And he ran and ran. I don't know, ever found out, how he came to Hamburg. If he went all the way to Hamburg by foot, if he took some-- because any-- afterwards, he came to Hamburg. And they put him into an orphan home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He died already. And I never had a chance to ask him. And my sister was in this house. So I was sitting the rest of the shiva with them in the house. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now, after the shiva was over, we tried to go back home to Bremen. My father and I went back home to Bremen, and my brother stayed in the orphan home. And we came back, and everything was mixed up. And that's where I found the little passport picture, which I showed you, of my mother, because my mother had made a passport pictures one week before the Kristallnacht, [INAUDIBLE] night. It seems they should call it [NON-ENGLISH]. That's how the Germans called it. Because we always wanted to go out and leave. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everything was [INAUDIBLE] was scratched. The pictures were down on the floor. Everything was upside down. You had to make your way even to get through there. And our bedroom was on the second floor, and we stayed there. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This was in November. The day after Kristallnacht, Goebbels, who was the propaganda minister in Germany, called it off. But as you know, the Jews had to pay for the damage which they did, and the insurance companies had to give them the money. So their business was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was just you and your father back In your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only I and my father went back home. And then I started to write letters again. Actually, my brother had bar mitzvah in beginning of October, 1938, a month before. And at that time, he had received a letter. At that day, I thought it was a present that we could leave. But the letter said we cannot do very much for you. But then when this Kristallnacht came, and even the United States, we called the ambassador for there-- from there. [CLEARS THROAT] We started writing again, and we wrote again to all the people whom we had written before, especially to my father's cousin in Canada, that-- what happened. And this time, we got a positive answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever report to the Gestapo in Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you see, [CLEARS THROAT] I just went in-- it was actually not the Gestapo, the police department.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I reported to the police department, that was it. But as time went on, in December, some people came back from the concentration camps. And they formed again a kind of congregation, and they organized themselves. And I also helped organize themselves. They started again with the Jewish services and different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I helped out. And then about January, the Gestapo said that all the Jewish congregation have to send a representative, who has to come once a week or even more often, and to report to them about the Jewish community. So I was appointed. I didn't volunteer. [LAUGHS] Were you appointed by the Jewish community or the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by the Jewish community. They had to appoint someone to go to the Gestapo. So I went to the Gestapo. But [CLEARS THROAT] before that, many things happened. Even in 1938-- I don't want to mix up the dates that much. Yet when things got worse, especially after Czechoslovakia and Austria was invaded in 1938, [CLEARS THROAT] then the Jews from the smaller communities moved into the Bremen because they felt safer in a larger community. And there was no place for them to live. So one family, whom I know very well-- members of this family are still alive. They live in [INAUDIBLE] Brooklyn, and they live in [INAUDIBLE]-- [CLEARS THROAT] donated their house, which was a six story house, and many Jewish family moved in there. Maybe 20 families moved in this home. And it became the center of the Jewish community. It became the center for services and for immigration work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So getting back to when you were the liaison to the Gestapo from the Jewish community, what were your duties?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to tell them what they did. They had, for instance, a president at that time, and they had services. And the Jews were living there. Did any more people come? Does anyone-- I don't know what they really wanted. They just [LAUGHS] wanted-- wanted a connection with the Jewish community to make sure, you see, you have everyone, because they had lists of every block of every house where people were living. But my-- my intention was different than their intention, because my intention was to get people out of concentration camps in the beginning. Later on, I had a second kind of objective. But in the beginning, just to get them out of them concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the names of the people you dealt with in the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a certain Mr. Parchman, P-A-R-C-H-M-A-N. He was never brought to trial for what he did. I don't know why, and-- But I met him all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there two or three times a week, and this was a special building which was-- Bremen, and you have to know Bremen was a city, a medieval city, which had a trench around it and a wall. And the trench, and the 'trench' in German is called [GERMAN], [? W-A-L-L. ?] And [CLEARS THROAT] there was this house, and there was no sign on it what it was. There was nothing there, only a bell up on the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: I pushed the bell, and the door would open. Then when the door would open, there was a big stairway upstairs, going upstairs. And at the bottom of the stairway was a big picture of a person like this with two fingers, and the word 'schweigend.' 'Schwiegend' means to be silent. Don't talk. [LAUGHS] Then he said to me, come upstairs to room number so and so. And I went upstairs. There was Mr. Parchman sitting with his feet on the table. And of course, they addressed the Jews in a very nasty way. They said, jude, sit down.

speaker: interviewer
text: The very first time when I came up there, he said, here are a few things which we found and we saved, which we rescued from your parent's home. Rescued? They confiscated this. [CLEARS THROAT] So what was it? Well, a few pictures, there was a few small items, paraphernalia. So I said, can I have them? So he gave me a few, and said, that you can have. The rest they kept. I don't know how-- how he knew about it. So he must have gone in there, or they must have gone in there to find out, if they had any valuables in the building. So what was your relationship with-- with the Gestapo? How-- what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always knew what he wanted to know from me, and I always-- and he always knew what he wanted from me, and I always knew what I wanted from him. And I said to him in the beginning-- [CLEARS THROAT] sometimes he told me, so-- so many Jews are in the concentration camps now. And I said to him, can you let them out? I want my brother to get out. My brother's in Sachsenhausen He knew all this, you see, but they made believe they didn't know. Sometimes he let out a few people. Sometimes he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he let your brother out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know now if it was because of him or because of something else. Because in the meantime, my relatives had made contact with the Jewish hospital in Hamburg. And somehow, the doctor gave a certificate that he had to go to a hospital. And so he came. Later on, he was released to the hospital. I don't if it came because of the hospital, because of him, or because of both. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- what did the Jewish community think of your role?

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as you could help them in any way, you see, it was good for them. You see, because I did a lot of things for them, other things too. Because, even though I only got my teaching certificate, as I told you before, afterwards in lieu of a real teacher certificate, [LAUGHS] I gave some courses there. I helped with the services. I-- I instituted for the women, a course of some things like, like sewing and cooking. And I don't know how to cook. [LAUGHS] At that time, I didn't know. [LAUGHS] And other things like this, you see, and also a little bit of languages. You see, I-- I was the only Spanish teacher ever in the teacher's seminary. And when I once was supposed to start, it stopped. So [CLEARS THROAT] I did a lot of things there, you see. And I also helped with the HIAS, Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society. It's also called in Germany HIAS. And to get the people out and to see what they needed. They needed-- if they needed a-- a certificate from the community that they didn't owe any money, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say get people out, do you mean getting people out of the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, even to get out wasn't easy. You see, even if you wanted to get out of Germany, you needed to sign out at different places. You needed a medical certificate. You needed a certificate that you don't owe any money. You needed a certificate that you have given all your silver away and things like this sort. You need all kinds of certificates. And it wasn't always easy to get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you-- were you able to help people to get those? Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from the congregation, we gave out such certificates. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you have this job as liaison to the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not a paid job, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I didn't think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BLOWS NOSE] [LAUGHS] I worked with them until I-- until my family and I emigrated, actually, from Germany at the end of 19-- May, 1939. It was a very short time. But I went there two to three times a week, especially in the beginning. I think December, January, I went there two or three times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, until all the people were out, then I also had, as my objective, to make a Jewish school for children, because children were now completely out of the education system, the German education system. And I negotiated with them. And I said to him, we need a school for Jewish children. He said, what do you want and need a school for? I said to him, we need a school because [CLEARS THROAT] you want them out. And when they go out, they have to be able to get a job in the other country. They have to learn something. They have to know something. But he said, when-- what time of the day do you want it? I said, what time do you think we should-- we should have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said in the morning, you cannot have it, because in the morning German children are taught, and German children and Jewish children cannot be taught at the same time. I said, OK then, let's make the afternoon. In the afternoon, we have no place where you can have it. We couldn't have it in the community house because it was crowded. There was two many families there, they said. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked around myself. Till finally, I found a place not far from the [INAUDIBLE], Bremen [NON-ENGLISH] it's called. And not far from the hotel where Hitler had killed [NON-ENGLISH] in 1934, also in Bremen. And [CLEARS THROAT] I said, can we have this? He said, yes, you can have it, but only two or three times a week and in the afternoon. But I had a problem then, because we had to have teachers-- no teachers. So I just told a few merchants and people who were just coming back from concentration camps how to teach them. So we taught them little things.

speaker: interviewee
text: We taught them a little bit about Jewish things, about history, about having gymnastics, a little bit of English, a little bit of geography-- where they would be going to. And we had 42 children, 42 children from all parts around Bremen, the surroundings, boys and girls between 6 and 12 years old, I think. Some of them, I still met-- I met later on in New York. Two of them I met in New York, and I'm still in contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to travel outside of Bremen during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was asked by the community in [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] means where the raise-- or the river front flows into the North Sea. It's not far from Bremerhaven. And also Bremerhaven, to come there and to talk to them about immigration. So when I did this-- because I did a few translations for the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society-- Yeah, I went there. And I instituted some causes. Not too often, because I spent only two or three months there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to have special permission to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he knew. Parchman, Parchman knew. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to go to Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, once, that was the only time I ever went to Berlin. It was on January 18th, 1939. And the night before I had visited with the Grunberg family who had donated the house, and I was talking to them. And in the middle of that evening, I got a telegram. It was three words-- 'come here at once.' [GERMAN] in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was signed, and I didn't know who it was. But then I recalled it must have been one person, one member of my organization, the Agudat Israel. And I thought they have an office in Berlin. And I think-- and I asked the Grunbergs. I said, what do you think I should do? So they said, go there. So I went again. I went to the Bremen railway station, and it was again late at night, 12 o'clock. And this was an express train. And in the express train, you have a hallway on the side of the train. Inside, you can walk from one place to another, and then they have cabins in there. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was always afraid the Nazis might recognize me and throw me out, what they did with many people. And so I went to Berlin, and I arrived in Berlin in-- in the morning-- on the night of the 18th. It was a dark day. I remember it was 7 o'clock in the morning, and it was raining. And I knew where to go. I knew it from a map. And I had to go under the [NON-ENGLISH] gate. And there was a sign on the [NON-ENGLISH] gate. It says, 'Jews enter here at their own risk.' But I walked through quickly. It was dark. It was raining. You see, people didn't watch very much. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked through there, and I came to the building. [NON-ENGLISH], I don't remember the name, exact name, of the [NON-ENGLISH] the street. And I walked up there to-- on the fourth floor, they had their office. So I came in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said to me-- and they said to me, we-- we knew that you would be coming. And this is what we have to do. We have to write out as many letters that our friends have been accepted for [NON-ENGLISH] in the Baltic States, and give them to the Gestapo by today. Then they will release them.
